name: One Orchestrator Many Workflows Assessment Pipeline
description: Concurrent workflow corpus slice intended for a single orchestrator to multiplex.
outcome: One orchestrator can coordinate many active assessment workflows without losing subject linkage.

definition.process_instructions:
Run the workflow through the authored stages `one-orchestrator-validation`. Treat each stage as a distinct checkpoint of evidence and handoff quality: `one-orchestrator-validation` exists to Concurrent subjects are delivered and assessed cleanly.
Use these roles deliberately: `one-orchestrator-delivery-engineer` Implements delivery outputs across concurrent work items; `one-orchestrator-quality-assessor` Assesses concurrent subject outputs for quality risk; `one-orchestrator-compliance-assessor` Assesses concurrent subject outputs for compliance risk.
Happy path: start with the earliest unfinished stage, dispatch the best matching producing role for that stage, require a full handoff with concrete artifacts or evidence, then move to the next stage only when the current output is usable. When a later stage is primarily evaluative, dispatch the reviewer or assessor roles after the producing role has handed off; when a later stage is packaging or release-oriented, dispatch the coordinating role only after upstream evidence is complete.
Unhappy path: If any role reports missing evidence, partial delivery, request changes, rejection, blocking risk, or an escalation, keep the current subject open, route work back to the most relevant producing role, and require a fresh handoff before trying again. If two decisions conflict, preserve both in history, but route using the newest subject state and the most restrictive still-active finding. Do not skip a stage just because a later role believes it can improvise around incomplete upstream work. This workflow may share one orchestrator fleet with many sibling workflows. The orchestrator SHOULD keep steps concise, rely on durable handoffs, and avoid holding unnecessary active work while waiting on assessments.
Completion rules: finish only after the last required stage has a final, internally consistent handoff, the final package matches the current subject state, and every role named above has either contributed or been intentionally skipped for a clear reason grounded in the playbook. Stage-specific closure for this playbook: each `one-orchestrator-validation` work item stays open until `one-orchestrator-delivery-engineer`, `one-orchestrator-quality-assessor`, and `one-orchestrator-compliance-assessor` have either submitted current-subject handoffs that satisfy the `one-orchestrator-validation` goal or been intentionally skipped with a concrete reason grounded in the playbook. Do not fabricate approvals, assessments, escalations, or branch changes that the playbook does not actually justify, and do not treat stale findings as permission to advance new work. Guided closure rule: treat the authored flow as best-intent process guidance. Preferred review paths and stage order SHOULD be followed when they remain useful, but the orchestrator MAY waive a preferred step with a recorded reason when closure is still legally possible. Only explicit blockers grounded in the current subject state should keep work open; non-blocking deviations, redundant reviews, and stale advisory findings should be recorded as callouts instead of preventing closure.

columns.label: Planned
columns.description: Work is defined but not yet assigned.

columns.label: Blocked
columns.description: Work is blocked and needs intervention.

columns.label: Done
columns.description: The work item meets its acceptance criteria.

stages.name: one-orchestrator-validation
stages.goal: Concurrent subjects are delivered and assessed cleanly.
